 461→\n   462→    public boolean hasStore(final String name) {\n   463→        return evaluateConditionIsTrueForAnyBuilders(b -> b.hasStore(name));\n   464→    }\n   465→\n   466→    public boolean hasOffsetResetOverrides() {\n   467→        // Return true if using named topologies, as there may be named topologies added later which do have overrides\n   468→        return hasNamedTopologies() || evaluateConditionIsTrueForAnyBuilders(InternalTopologyBuilder::hasOffsetResetOverrides);\n   469→    }\n   470→\n   471→    public Optional<AutoOffsetResetStrategy> offsetResetStrategy(final String topic) {\n   472→        for (final InternalTopologyBuilder builder : builders.values()) {\n   473→            if (builder.containsTopic(topic)) {\n   474→                return Optional.ofNullable(builder.offsetResetStrategy(topic));\n   475→            }\n   476→        }\n   477→        log.warn(\"Unable to look up offset reset strategy for topic {} \" +\n   478→            \"as this topic does not appear in the sources of any of the current topologies: {}\\n \" +\n   479→                \"This may be due to natural race condition when removing a topology but it should not \" +\n   480→                \"persist or appear frequently.\",\n   481→            topic, namedTopologiesView()\n   482→        );\n   483→        // returning `null` for an Optional return type triggers spotbugs\n   484→        // we added an exception for NP_OPTIONAL_RETURN_NULL for this method\n   485→        // when we remove NamedTopologies, we can remove this exception\n   486→        return null;\n   487→    }\n   488→\n   489→\n   490→    public Collection<String> fullSourceTopicNamesForTopology(final String topologyName) {\n   491→        Objects.requireNonNull(topologyName, \"topology name must not be null\");\n   492→        return lookupBuilderForNamedTopology(topologyName).fullSourceTopicNames();\n   493→    }\n   494→\n   495→    public Collection<String> allFullSourceTopicNames() {\n   496→        final List<String> sourceTopics = new ArrayList<>();\n   497→        applyToEachBuilder(b -> sourceTopics.addAll(b.fullSourceTopicNames()));\n   498→        return sourceTopics;\n   499→    }\n   500→\n   501→    Pattern sourceTopicPattern() {\n   502→        final StringBuilder patternBuilder = new StringBuilder();\n   503→\n   504→        applyToEachBuilder(b -> {\n   505→            final String patternString = b.sourceTopicPatternString();\n   506→            if (!patternString.isEmpty()) {\n   507→                patternBuilder.append(patternString).append(\"|\");\n   508→            }\n   509→        });\n   510→\n   511→        if (patternBuilder.length() > 0) {\n   512→            patternBuilder.setLength(patternBuilder.length() - 1);\n   513→            return Pattern.compile(patternBuilder.toString());\n   514→        } else {\n   515→            return EMPTY_ZERO_LENGTH_PATTERN;\n   516→        }\n   517→    }\n   518→\n   519→    public boolean usesPatternSubscription() {\n   520→        return }\n   164→\n   165→    synchronized void put(final Bytes key, final LRUCacheEntry value) {\n   166→        if (!value.isDirty() && dirtyKeys.contains(key)) {\n   167→            throw new           hitRatioSensor.record((double) numReadHits / (double) (numReadHits + numReadMisses));\n   209→        }\n   210→        return node;\n   211→    }\n   212→\n   213→    private void {\n   226→            node.next.previous = node.previous;\n   227→        } else {\n   228→            tail = node.previous;\n   229→        }\n   230→    }\n   231→\n   232→    private void == null) {\n   246→            return;\n   247→        }\n   248→        final LRUNode eldest = tail;\n   249→        currentSizeBytes -= eldest.size();\n   250→        remove(eldest);\n   251→ 313→        } else {\n   314→            return cache.navigableKeySet().subSet(from, true, to, toInclusive);\n   315→        }\n   316→    }\n   317→\n   318→    private Iterator<Bytes> public KafkaFuture<Void> lockTasks(final Set<TaskId> taskIds) {\n   216→        final KafkaFutureImpl<Void> result = new KafkaFutureImpl<>();\n   217→\n   218→        if (taskIds.isEmpty()) {\n   219→            result.complete(null);\n   220→            return result;\n   221→        }\n   222→\n   223→        return returnWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n   224→            lockedTasks.addAll(taskIds);\n tasksLock.unlock();\n   379→        }\n   380→    }\n   381→\n   382→    private <T> T returnWithTasksLocked(final Supplier<T> action) {\n   383→        tasksLock.lock();\n   384→        try {\n Now I need to check if the same signaling happens for exceptions and failed tasks in `drainExceptionsAndFailedTasks`.\n\nChecking exception handling...\n\nThe `drainExceptionsAndFailedTasks` method just locks, drains the list, and returns without any `await()` calls, so signaling isn't necessary there. But when tasks are added to the queue through the `add()` method, it does signal the condition variable after adding to the task queue.\n\nNow I'm going to write tests and run Fray to verify everything works. Possibly involving callback/listener patterns that cross lock boundaries.\n\n2. **A lost wakeup / hang** in one of the condition variable patterns. Possibly in `TopologyMetadata.maybeWaitForNonEmptyTopology` where the thread can wait forever if the topology never becomes non-empty and no signal is sent.\n\n3. **A deadlock** in `CachingKeyValueStore` between the read/write lock and the synchronized(position) block, in some edge case I didn't consider.\n\nLet me try to write Fray tests for these and see what happens. I'll start with the simplest, most likely bugs.\n\nActually, on further reflection, let me re-examine the CachingKeyValueStore one more time with a focus on the `query` method. I'm tracing through the lock acquisition order: `query` grabs the read lock then enters a synchronized block on position, while `put` acquires the write lock before entering that same synchronized block. Now I'm seeing a potential cycle where `getPosition` called externally could hold the position lock while waiting for another lock, `put` could be blocked waiting for position while holding the write lock, and `query` could be blocked trying to acquire the read lock because `put` is in the way. This looks like a genuine deadlock scenario. I'm wondering if `wrappedPosition` could be held by another thread—but since it's the return value from `wrapped().getPosition()` called before any synchronized blocks, it should be a completed object unless the wrapped store returns a shared Position instance that other threads also synchronize on. If the wrapped store has its own `position` field and returns it from `getPosition()`, then other threads operating on that wrapped store would be synchronizing on the same object in their own `putInternal` calls.\n\nThe scenario gets complex with nested CachingStores wrapping the same underlying store—if Thread B is another CachingStore doing its own `getPosition()` call, it might end up synchronizing on the same underlying position object, but that alone wouldn't create a deadlock since each thread would just wait its turn on that lock.\n\nLet me stop overthinking this and write the tests. But first, I need to verify something critical about the `wrapped().getPosition()` call—specifically, whether the wrapped store returns its position field directly or a copy, since that determines whether `wrappedPosition` in `CachingKeyValueStore.getPosition()` is actually a mutable shared reference to the wrapped store's position object.\n\nNow I'm tracing through the synchronization scenarios: if the wrapped store returns its position field directly, then Thread A synchronizing on `this.position` would eventually synchronize on the same object that Thread B is using when it accesses the wrapped store, creating contention but not deadlock. However, if the wrapped store is itself a `CachingKeyValueStore`, it returns a fresh merged position object, so there's no contention at all. The real concern is the non-caching case where multiple threads could be accessing the wrapped store's position field concurrently.\n\nActually, I think I've convinced myself there's no deadlock risk here—Thread A would hold the caching store's position lock and then try to acquire the wrapped store's position lock, while Thread B would only hold the wrapped store's position lock with no reason to ever need the caching store's lock.\n\nNow I need to write a test to verify this behavior. It acquires the write lock and then calls `cache().flush()`, which could potentially trigger other operations that might interact with the lock in unexpected ways.\n\nThe flush listener could invoke `putAndMaybeForward`, which then calls the wrapped store's `put()` method. If that wrapped store tries to acquire a lock while this thread is holding the write lock, and another thread is waiting for this write lock while holding the wrapped store's lock, we'd have a deadlock. But since the wrapped store doesn't have visibility into this store's locking mechanism, that scenario seems unlikely unless there's some callback architecture I'm missing.\n\nLet me look at the `get()` method now. It appears to have logic that determines which lock to acquire based on whether the current thread is the stream thread.\n\nThe stream thread acquires the write lock because `getInternal` might modify the cache by adding an LRU entry. Other threads only need the read lock since they can't modify the cache. This means the stream thread's `get()` calls will block any concurrent read operations from other threads.\n\nNow I'm realizing something important about the `putAll()` method that I need to examine more carefully. The `synchronized(position)` block in `putInternal` is necessary because `getPosition()` is a public method that can be called without holding the read/write lock, so both methods need to synchronize on the `position` field to prevent race conditions. This creates two layers of protection: the ReadWriteLock coordinates `put`/`get`/`query` operations, while the `synchronized(position)` block ensures mutual exclusion specifically for the `position` field between `putInternal` and direct `getPosition` calls.\n\nNow I'm checking whether these two locking mechanisms could create a deadlock by tracing the lock acquisition order across different threads—specifically looking for a cycle where one thread holds a lock another needs while waiting for something the first thread holds.\n\nThe issue is that for a cycle to form, Thread C would need to hold `wrappedPosition` and want our `writeLock`, but that would require Thread C to call `put()` on this store while operating on the wrapped store, which doesn't happen since the `CacheFlushListener` only goes one direction.\n\nSo the code appears deadlock-free, but examining the broader codebase reveals the real vulnerability: `DefaultStateUpdater`'s `executeWithQueuesLocked` method chains multiple lock acquisitions...\n\nActually, let me trace through the interaction between `drainRestoredActiveTasks` and `executeWithQueuesLocked` more carefully to see if there's a hidden cycle I'm missing. }\n\n    public long evicts() {\n        return numEvicts;\n    }\n\n    public long flushes() {\n        return numFlushes;\n    }\n\n    public synchronized void resize(final long newCacheSizeBytes) {\n        final boolean shrink = newCacheSizeBytes < maxCacheSizeBytes;\n        maxCacheSizeBytes = newCacheSizeBytes;\n        if (shrink) {\n            log.debug(\"Cache size was shrunk to {}\", newCacheSizeBytes);\n            if (caches.values().isEmpty()) {\n                return;\n            }\n            final CircularIterator<NamedCache> circularIterator = new CircularIterator<>(caches.values());\n            while (sizeInBytes.get() > maxCacheSizeBytes) {\n                final NamedCache cache = circularIterator.next();\n                synchronized (cache) {\n                    final long oldSize = cache.sizeInBytes();\n                    cache.evict();\n                    sizeInBytes.getAndAdd(cache.sizeInBytes() - oldSize);\n                }\n                numEvicts++;\n            }\n        } else {\n            log.debug(\"Cache size was expanded to {}\", newCacheSizeBytes);\n        }\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * The thread cache oldSize);\n            maybeEvict(namespace, cache);\n        }\n\n        if (result == null) {\n            numPuts++;\n        }\n        return result;\n    }\n\n    public void putAll(final this.metrics));\n        }\n        return new MemoryLRUCacheBytesIterator(cache.keyRange(from, to, toInclusive), cache);\n    }\n\n    public MemoryLRUCacheBytesIterator reverseRange(final String    public long size() {\n        long size = 0;\n        for (final NamedCache cache : caches.values()) {\n            size += cache.size();\n            if (isOverflowing(size)) {\n  return sizeInBytes.get();\n    }\n\n    synchronized void close(final String namespace) {\n        final NamedCache removed = caches.remove(namespace);\n        if (removed != null) {\n String namespace, final NamedCache cache) {\n        int numEvicted = 0;\n        while (sizeInBytes.get() > maxCacheSizeBytes) {\n            // we abort here as the put on this cache may have triggered\n            // a put on another cache. So even though the sizeInBytes() is\n            // still > maxCacheSizeBytes there is nothing to evict from this\n            // namespaced cache.\n            if (cache.isEmpty()) {\n                return;\n            }\n            final long oldSize = cache.sizeInBytes();\n            cache.evict();\n            sizeInBytes.getAndAdd(cache.sizeInBytes() - oldSize);\n            numEvicts++;\n NamedCache cache = caches.get(name);\n        if (cache == null) {\n            cache = new NamedCache(name, this.metrics);\n            caches.put(name, cache);\n        }\n        return cache;\n    }\n\n    static class MemoryLRUCacheBytesIterator implements PeekingKeyValueIterator<Bytes, LRUCacheEntry> {\n        private final Iterator<Bytes> keys;\n        private final NamedCache cache;\n        private KeyValue<Bytes, LRUCacheEntry> nextEntry;\n\n        MemoryLRUCacheBytesIterator(final Iterator<Bytes> keys, final NamedCache cache) {\n            this.keys = keys;\n            this.cache = cache;\n        }\n\n        public Bytes peekNextKey() {\n            if (!hasNext()) {\n                throw new NoSuchElementException();\n            }\n            return nextEntry.key;\n        }\n\n\n        public KeyValue<Bytes, LRUCacheEntry> peekNext() {\n            if (!hasNext()) {\n                throw new NoSuchElementException();\n            }\n            return nextEntry;\n        }\n\n        @Override\n        public boolean hasNext() {\n            if (nextEntry != null) {\n                return true;\n            }\n\n            while (keys.hasNext() && nextEntry == null) {\n                internalNext();\n            }\n\n            return nextEntry != null;\n        }\n\n        @Override\n        public KeyValue<Bytes, LRUCacheEntry> next() {\n            if (!hasNext()) {\n                throw new NoSuchElementException();\n            }\n            final KeyValue<Bytes, LRUCacheEntry> result = nextEntry;\n            nextEntry = null;\n            return result;\n        }\n\n        private void internalNext() {\n            final Bytes cacheKey = keys.next();\n            final LRUCacheEntry entry = cache.get(cacheKey);\n            if (entry == null) {\n                return;\n            }\n\n            nextEntry = new KeyValue<>(cacheKey, entry);\n        }\n\n        @Override\n        public void close() {\n            // do {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_01F546h5DKrwfuUNJyQCu1aE","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Interesting - TaskManager.java has no locks at all. Let me focus on the ThreadCache pattern.\n\nIn `ThreadCache`:\n- `resize()`: `synchronized(this)` -> `synchronized(cache)` (lines 80-101)\n- `flush()`: `synchronized(cache)` (lines 146-150)\n- `put()`: `synchronized(cache)` (lines 176-181)\n- `close()`: `synchronized(this)` (line 276)\n- `clear()`: `synchronized(this)` (line 284)\n- `getCache()`: `synchronized(this)` (line 313)\n\n\n- `getOrCreateCache()`: `synchronized(this)` (line 317)\n\nNow I'm seeing a potential issue: when `flush()` acquires the lock on `cache` and calls `cache.flush()`, the NamedCache can trigger its flush listener, which was registered in `CachingKeyValueStore.init()` to iterate through dirty entries and call `putAndMaybeForward()` for each one. This means the flush operation could invoke downstream logic while holding the cache lock.\n\nThe problem compounds when `put()` also acquires `synchronized(cache)` and calls `maybeEvict()`, which can trigger `cache.evict()`. During eviction, if dirty entries need flushing, the flush listener fires again—still under the same cache lock—and calls `putAndMaybeForward()` recursively. Meanwhile, `resize()` acquires `synchronized(this)` first, then `synchronized(cache)`, whereas `put()` only acquires the cache lock directly after releasing the `this` lock in `getOrCreateCache()`. This creates a potential deadlock scenario depending on the lock acquisition order. ).\n\nWait, let me trace through `put()` more carefully. The `getOrCreateCache(namespace)` call acquires and releases the `this` lock, then returns the cache object. After that, `synchronized(cache)` acquires the cache lock. So the lock ordering is sequential: `this` releases before `cache` is acquired—they're not nested.\n\nIn contrast, `resize()` does nest the locks: it holds `this` while acquiring `cache`. And `flush()` only uses the `cache` lock.\n\nSo the lock patterns are:\n- `resize()`: `this` -> `cache` (nested)\n- `put()`: `this` (release) -> `cache` (sequential)\n- `flush()`: `cache` only\n\nThere's no reverse ordering and no other method does `cache` -> `this`, so deadlock shouldn't happen. But I'm realizing that `maybeEvict()` within `put()` calls `cache.evict()`, which could trigger a flush of dirty entries. The flush listener then calls back into the CachingKeyValueStore, which invokes `wrapped().put()`. If the wrapped store is the underlying store and its `put()` method somehow calls back into ThreadCache for a different namespace, we'd have a chain like: `synchronized(cache1)` -> `maybeEvict` -> `cache1.evict()` -> `flushListener` -> `CachingKVStore.putAndMaybeForward` -> `wrapped.put()` -> potentially `ThreadCache.put(ns2)`. That second `put()` would then do `getOrCreateCache(ns2)` which acquires `synchronized(this)`. So we'd end up with `synchronized(cache1)` -> `synchronized(this)`. Combined with `resize()` doing `synchronized(this)` -> `synchronized(cache)`, if that cache happens to be `cache1`, we could have a deadlock scenario.\n\nThread A holds `cache1` and waits for `this`, while Thread B holds `this` and waits for `cache1` — a classic circular wait. The question is whether the eviction and flush listener path can actually trigger a callback back into ThreadCache, which I need to trace through carefully. the flush listener calls `putAndMaybeForward`, which retrieves the old value from the wrapped store if needed and then puts the new value—all operations on the underlying store, not back through the cache. So there's no recursive callback to ThreadCache during eviction. The same logic holds for the `resize()` path that triggers eviction.\n\nBut I'm realizing there's a potential deadlock scenario if we have nested CachingKeyValueStore instances: when one store's flush listener calls `put()` on a wrapped CachingKeyValueStore, that wrapped store would try to call back into ThreadCache, which could deadlock if ThreadCache is already holding the lock during its own flush operation. I'm questioning whether this scenario is even realistic—in standard Kafka Streams, you'd typically see CachingKeyValueStore wrapping a non-caching store like RocksDB, not another CachingKeyValueStore, so this particular deadlock path might not actually occur in practice.\n\nLooking at the `put` method more carefully, I see that `getOrCreateCache` acquires the lock briefly to get or create the cache, then releases it before the actual put operation happens on the individual cache object, so there's no nested locking issue there either. So the eviction callback doesn't actually trigger a put back into ThreadCache in standard Kafka Streams—it just flushes to the underlying store like RocksDB. That rules out that deadlock scenario. So interrupting the thread will wake it up with an InterruptedException, and the catch block re-checks the while condition. The hang scenario occurs when a thread is in `topologyCV.await()` with the lock released, its state becomes non-alive, but no one signals the condition or interrupts it—so it stays waiting forever. For this to not be a bug, the shutdown path must either signal `topologyCV` or interrupt the thread.\n\nNow I'm looking at where the lost wakeup might occur. private void validateTopologyConsistency(final Set<String> activeTopologies) {\n   110→        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n   111→            for (final StreamTask task : assign is unrecognized\");\n   125→            }\n   126→\n   127→            // the most naive scheduling algorithm for now: give the next unlocked, unassigned, and  processable task\n   128→ {\n   155→            for (final StreamTask task : tasks.activeInitializedTasks()) {\n   156→                if (!assignedTasks.containsKey(task.id()) &&\n   157→                    !lockedTasks.contains(task.id()) &&\n   158→                    canProgress(task, time.milliseconds()) &&\n   159→                    !hasUncaughtException(task.id())\n   160→                ) {\n   161→                    log.debug(\"Await unblocked: returning early from await since a processable task {} was found\", task.id());\n   162→                    return false;\n   163→                }\n   164→            }\n   165→            try {\n   166→                // We re-check the shutdownRequest atomic boolean to avoid a race condition. If this thread was\n   167→                // previously interrupted while awaiting tasksCondition, it is possible to miss the signalAll that\n   168→                // is called during shutdown. 193→    }\n   194→\n   195→    @Override\n   196→    public void unassignTask(final StreamTask task, final TaskExecutor executor) {\n   197→        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n   198→ public KafkaFuture<Void> lockTasks(final Set<TaskId> taskIds) {\n   216→        final KafkaFutureImpl<Void> result = new KafkaFutureImpl<>();\n   217→\n   218→        if (taskIds.isEmpty()) {\n   219→            result.complete(null);\n   220→            return result;\n   221→        }\n   222→\n   223→        return returnWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n   224→            lockedTasks.addAll(taskIds);\n executeWithTasksLocked(final Runnable action) {\n   374→        tasksLock.lock();\n   375→        try {\n   376→            action.run();\n   377→        } finally {\n   378→            tasksLock.unlock();\n   379→        }\n   380→    }\n   381→\n   382→    private <T> T returnWithTasksLocked(final Supplier<T> action) {\n   383→        tasksLock.lock();\n   384→        try {\n   385→            return action.get();\n   386→        } finally {\n   387→            tasksLock.unlock();\n   388→        }\n   389→    }\n   390→\n   391→    private boolean canProgress(final taskExecutionMetadata.canPunctuateTask(task) && (task.canPunctuateStreamTime() || task.canPunctuateSystemTime());\n   395→    }\n   396→\n   397→    public void startTaskExecutors() {\n   398→ It's assigned to one of the executors for processing.\n * 2. It's locked for committing, removal, other manipulations etc.\n * 3. Neither 1 or 2, i.e. it stays idle. This is possible if we do not have enough executors or because those tasks\n *    are not processable (e.g. because no records fetched) yet.\n */\npublic final class DefaultTaskManager implements TaskManager {\n\n    private final Time time;\n    private final Logger log;\n    private final TasksRegistry tasks;\n    private final TopologyMetadata topologyMetadata;\n\n    private final Lock tasksLock = new ReentrantLock();\n    private final Condition tasksCondition = tasksLock.newCondition();\n    private final List<TaskId> lockedTasks = new ArrayList<>();\n    private final Map<TaskId, StreamsException> uncaughtExceptions = new HashMap<>();\n    private final Map<TaskId, TaskExecutor> assignedTasks = new HashMap<>();\n    private final TaskExecutionMetadata taskExecutionMetadata;\n\n    private final List<TaskExecutor> taskExecutors;\n\n    public static class DefaultTaskExecutorCreator implements TaskExecutorCreator {\n        @Override\n        public TaskExecutor create(final TaskManager taskManager, final String name, final Time time, final TaskExecutionMetadata taskExecutionMetadata) {\n            return new DefaultTaskExecutor(taskManager, name, time, taskExecutionMetadata);\n        }\n    }\n\n    public DefaultTaskManager(final Time time,\n                              final String clientId,\n                              final TasksRegistry tasks,\n                              final TaskExecutorCreator executorCreator,\n                              final TaskExecutionMetadata taskExecutionMetadata,\n                              final int numExecutors,\n                              final TopologyMetadata topologyMetadata\n                              ) {\n        final String logPrefix = String.format(\"%s \", clientId);\n        final LogContext logContext = new LogContext(logPrefix);\n        this.log = logContext.logger(DefaultTaskManager.class);\n        this.time = time;\n        this.tasks = tasks;\n        this.taskExecutionMetadata = taskExecutionMetadata;\n        this.topologyMetadata = topologyMetadata;\n        topologyMetadata.registerLifecycleObserver(new TaskTopologyObserver());\n\n        this.taskExecutors = new ArrayList<>(numExecutors);\n        for (int i = 1; i <= numExecutors; i++) {\n            final String name = clientId + \"-TaskExecutor-\" + i;\n            this.taskExecutors.add(executorCreator.create(this, name, time, taskExecutionMetadata));\n        }\n    }\n\n    private class TaskTopologyObserver implements TopologyMetadata.TopologyLifecycleObserver {\n        @Override\n        public void onTopologyChange(final long newVersion, final Set<String> activeTopologies) {\n            validateTopologyConsistency(activeTopologies);\n        }\n    }\n\n    private void validateTopologyConsistency(final Set<String> activeTopologies) {\n        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            for (final StreamTask task : tasks.activeInitializedTasks()) {\n                final String topologyName = task.id().topologyName();\n                if (topologyName != null && !activeTopologies.contains(topologyName)) {\n                    log.warn(\"Task {} belongs to topology {} which is no longer active\", task.id(), topologyName);\n                }\n            }\n        });\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public StreamTask assignNextTask(final TaskExecutor executor) {\n        return returnWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            if (!taskExecutors.contains(executor)) {\n                throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"The requested executor for getting next task to assign is unrecognized\");\n            }\n\n            // the most naive scheduling algorithm for now: give the next unlocked, unassigned, and  processable task\n            for (final StreamTask task : tasks.activeInitializedTasks()) {\n                if (topologyMetadata.isTaskTopologyStale(task.id())) {\n                    continue;\n                }\n                if (!assignedTasks.containsKey(task.id()) &&\n                    !lockedTasks.contains(task.id()) &&\n                    canProgress(task, time.milliseconds()) &&\n                    !hasUncaughtException(task.id())\n                ) {\n\n                    assignedTasks.put(task.id(), executor);\n\n                    log.debug(\"Assigned task {} to executor {}\", task.id(), executor.name());\n\n                    return task;\n                }\n            }\n\n            log.debug(\"Found no assignable task for executor {}\", executor.name());\n\n            return null;\n        });\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void awaitProcessableTasks(final Supplier<Boolean> isShuttingDown) throws InterruptedException {\n        final boolean interrupted = returnWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            for (final StreamTask task : tasks.activeInitializedTasks()) {\n                if (!assignedTasks.containsKey(task.id()) &&\n                    !lockedTasks.contains(task.id()) &&\n                    canProgress(task, time.milliseconds()) &&\n                    !hasUncaughtException(task.id())\n                ) {\n                    log.debug(\"Await unblocked: returning early from await since a processable task {} was found\", task.id());\n                    return false;\n                }\n            }\n            try {\n                // We re-check the shutdownRequest atomic boolean to avoid a race condition. If this thread was\n                // previously interrupted while awaiting tasksCondition, it is possible to miss the signalAll that\n                // is called during shutdown. If this happens, we end up blocking in the await forever.\n                if (!isShuttingDown.get()) {\n                    log.debug(\"Await blocking\");\n                    tasksCondition.await();\n                } else {\n                    log.debug(\"Not awaiting since shutdown was requested\");\n                }\n            } catch (final InterruptedException ignored) {\n                // we interrupt the thread for shut down and pause.\n                // we can ignore this exception.\n                log.debug(\"Await unblocked: Interrupted while waiting for processable tasks\");\n                return true;\n            }\n            log.debug(\"Await unblocked: Woken up to check for processable tasks\");\n            return false;\n        });\n\n        if (interrupted) {\n            throw new InterruptedException();\n        }\n    }\n\n    public void signalTaskExecutors() {\n        log.debug(\"Waking up task executors\");\n        executeWithTasksLocked(tasksCondition::signalAll);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void unassignTask(final StreamTask task, final TaskExecutor executor) {\n        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            if (!taskExecutors.contains(executor)) {\n                throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"The requested executor for unassign task is unrecognized\");\n            }\n\n            final TaskExecutor lockedExecutor = assignedTasks.get(task.id());\n            if (lockedExecutor == null || lockedExecutor != executor) {\n                throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"Task \" + task.id() + \" is not locked by the executor\");\n            }\n\n            assignedTasks.remove(task.id());\n\n            log.debug(\"Unassigned {} from executor {}\", task.id(), executor.name());\n            tasksCondition.signalAll();\n        });\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public KafkaFuture<Void> lockTasks(final Set<TaskId> taskIds) {\n        final KafkaFutureImpl<Void> result = new KafkaFutureImpl<>();\n\n        if (taskIds.isEmpty()) {\n            result.complete(null);\n            return result;\n        }\n\n        return returnWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            lockedTasks.addAll(taskIds);\n\n            final Set<TaskId> remainingTaskIds = new ConcurrentSkipListSet<>(taskIds);\n\n            for (final TaskId taskId : taskIds) {\n                final Task task = tasks.initializedTask(taskId);\n\n                if (task == null) {\n                    throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"Trying to lock task \" + taskId + \" but it's not owned\");\n                }\n\n                if (!task.isActive()) {\n                    throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"The locking task \" + taskId + \" is not an active task\");\n                }\n\n                if (assignedTasks.containsKey(taskId)) {\n                    final TaskExecutor executor = assignedTasks.get(taskId);\n                    log.debug(\"Requesting release of task {} from {}\", taskId, executor.name());\n                    final KafkaFuture<StreamTask> future = executor.unassign();\n                    future.whenComplete((streamTask, throwable) -> {\n                        if (throwable != null) {\n                            result.completeExceptionally(throwable);\n                        } else {\n                            assert !assignedTasks.containsKey(taskId);\n                            // It can happen that the executor handed back the task before we asked it to\n                            // in which case `streamTask` will be null here.\n                            assert streamTask == null || streamTask.id() == taskId;\n                            remainingTaskIds.remove(taskId);\n                            if (remainingTaskIds.isEmpty()) {\n                                result.complete(null);\n                            }\n                        }\n                    });\n                } else {\n                    remainingTaskIds.remove(taskId);\n                    if (remainingTaskIds.isEmpty()) {\n                        result.complete(null);\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n\n            return result;\n        });\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public KafkaFuture<Void> lockAllTasks() {\n        return returnWithTasksLocked(() ->\n            lockTasks(tasks.activeInitializedTasks().stream().map(Task::id).collect(Collectors.toSet()))\n        );\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void unlockTasks(final Set<TaskId> taskIds) {\n\n        if (taskIds.isEmpty()) {\n            return;\n        }\n\n        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            lockedTasks.removeAll(taskIds);\n            log.debug(\"Waking up task executors\");\n            tasksCondition.signalAll();\n        });\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void unlockAllTasks() {\n        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> unlockTasks(tasks.activeInitializedTasks().stream().map(Task::id).collect(Collectors.toSet())));\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void add(final Set<StreamTask> tasksToAdd) {\n        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            for (final StreamTask task : tasksToAdd) {\n                tasks.addActiveTask(task);\n            }\n            log.debug(\"Waking up task executors\");\n            tasksCondition.signalAll();\n        });\n\n        log.info(\"Added tasks {} to the task manager to process\", tasksToAdd);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void remove(final TaskId taskId) {\n        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            if (assignedTasks.containsKey(taskId)) {\n                throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"The task to remove is still assigned to executors\");\n            }\n\n            if (!lockedTasks.contains(taskId)) {\n                throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"The task to remove is not locked yet by the task manager\");\n            }\n\n            if (!tasks.containsInitialized(taskId)) {\n                throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"The task to remove is not owned by the task manager\");\n            }\n\n            tasks.removeTask(tasks.initializedTask(taskId));\n        });\n\n        log.info(\"Removed task {} from the task manager\", taskId);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public Set<ReadOnlyTask> getTasks() {\n        return returnWithTasksLocked(() -> tasks.activeInitializedTasks().stream().map(ReadOnlyTask::new).collect(Collectors.toSet()));\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void setUncaughtException(final StreamsException exception, final TaskId taskId) {\n        executeWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n\n            if (!assignedTasks.containsKey(taskId)) {\n                throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"An uncaught exception can only be set as long as the task is still assigned\");\n            }\n\n            if (uncaughtExceptions.containsKey(taskId)) {\n                throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"The uncaught exception must be cleared before restarting processing\");\n            }\n\n            uncaughtExceptions.put(taskId, exception);\n        });\n\n        log.info(\"Set an uncaught exception of type {} for task {}, with error message: {}\",\n            exception.getClass().getName(),\n            taskId,\n            exception.getMessage());\n    }\n\n    public Map<TaskId, RuntimeException> drainUncaughtExceptions() {\n        final Map<TaskId, RuntimeException> returnValue = returnWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n            final Map<TaskId, RuntimeException> result = new HashMap<>(uncaughtExceptions);\n            uncaughtExceptions.clear();\n            return result;\n        });\n\n        if (!returnValue.isEmpty()) {\n            log.debug(\"Drained {} uncaught exceptions\", returnValue.size());\n        }\n\n        return returnValue;\n    }\n\n    public boolean hasUncaughtException(final TaskId taskId) {\n        return returnWithTasksLocked(() -> uncaughtExceptions.containsKey(taskId));\n    }\n\n    private void executeWithTasksLocked(final Runnable action) {\n        tasksLock.lock();\n        try {\n            action.run();\n        } finally {\n            tasksLock.unlock();\n        }\n    }\n\n    private <T> T returnWithTasksLocked(final Supplier<T> action) {\n        tasksLock.lock();\n        try {\n            return action.get();\n        } finally {\n            tasksLock.unlock();\n        }\n    }\n\n    private boolean canProgress(final StreamTask task, final long nowMs) {\n        return\n            taskExecutionMetadata.canProcessTask(task, nowMs) && task.isProcessable(nowMs) ||\n                taskExecutionMetadata.canPunctuateTask(task) && (task.canPunctuateStreamTime() || task.canPunctuateSystemTime());\n    }\n\n    public void startTaskExecutors() {\n        for (final TaskExecutor t: taskExecutors) {\n            t.start();\n        }\n    }\n\n    public void shutdown(final Duration duration) {\n        for (final TaskExecutor t: taskExecutors) {\n            t.requestShutdown();\n        }\n        signalTaskExecutors();\n        for (final TaskExecutor t: taskExecutors) {\n            t.awaitShutdown(duration);\n        }\n    }\n}\n","numLines":413,"startLine":1,"totalLines":413}}}

Let me look at the `awaitProcessableTasks` method more carefully:\n\n```java\npublic void awaitProcessableTasks(final Supplier<Boolean> isShuttingDown) throws InterruptedException {\n    final boolean interrupted = returnWithTasksLocked(() -> {\n        for (final StreamTask task : tasks.activeInitializedTasks()) {\n            if (!assignedTasks.containsKey(task.id()) &&\n                !lockedTasks.contains(task.id()) &&\n                canProgress(task, time.milliseconds()) &&\n                !hasUncaughtException(task.id())\n            ) {\n                return false;  // processable task found, don't wait\n            }\n        }\n        try {\n            if (!isShuttingDown.get()) {\n                tasksCondition.await();  // WAIT\n            } else {\n                log.debug(\"Not awaiting since shutdown was requested\");\n            }\n        } catch (final InterruptedException ignored) {\n            return true;\n        }\n        return false;\n    });\n    if (interrupted) {\n        throw new InterruptedException();\n    }\n}\n```\n\nSo the method loops through active tasks checking if any are processable (not assigned, not locked, can make progress, and have no uncaught exceptions), and if one is found it returns immediately. Otherwise it waits on a condition variable unless shutdown is requested, catching interruptions and propagating them up.\n\nThe comment flags a potential race condition: if this thread gets interrupted while waiting on the condition, it might miss the signalAll broadcast during shutdown, causing it to block forever. The code tries to prevent this by checking the shutdown flag before awaiting, but I need to verify if that's actually sufficient.\n\nLooking at `lockAllTasks`, it wraps a call to `lockTasks` which itself calls `returnWithTasksLocked` again, creating a nested locking pattern.  Since the lock is reentrant, this works fine—the hold count increments and decrements properly. But now I'm wondering what happens in `unlockAllTasks` when it tries to unwind these nested acquisitions. So the lifecycle observer creates a lock ordering where `topologyLock` is acquired first, then `tasksLock` inside the observer callback. Now I need to check if there's a reverse ordering anywhere—whether any code path acquires `tasksLock` before `topologyLock`.\n\nLooking at `assignNextTask`, it holds `tasksLock` and then calls `isTaskTopologyStale`, which acquires `topologyLock`. That's the reverse ordering right there—`tasksLock` first, then `topologyLock`.\n\nBut `registerAndBuildNewTopology` goes through `validateTopologyConsistency` and does it the opposite way: `topologyLock` first, then `tasksLock`. This is a classic deadlock scenario where Thread A waits for `topologyLock` while holding `tasksLock`, and Thread B waits for `tasksLock` while holding `topologyLock`.\n\nNow I'm tracing through the actual execution paths to confirm the deadlock. Thread A in `assignNextTask` acquires `tasksLock`, then tries to check if the task topology is stale which requires `topologyLock`. Meanwhile, Thread B in the topology update path acquires `topologyLock` first, notifies observers about the change, and eventually needs `tasksLock` in `validateTopologyConsistency`. They're waiting on each other in a circular dependency.\n\nLet me look at the `awaitProcessableTasks` method to see if there's also a missed signal issue...\n\nThe method checks for processable tasks while holding `tasksLock`, and if none are found, it waits on `tasksCondition`. The comment reveals the real problem: if this thread gets interrupted while waiting, it might miss the `signalAll` that happens during shutdown, creating a lost wakeup scenario.\n\nThe `isShuttingDown` check before entering `await()` is meant to prevent this—if shutdown was already requested, don't wait. But I'm seeing there might still be a race condition in how the shutdown path actually signals the condition.\n\nLooking at the shutdown sequence: the shutdown thread requests shutdown on each executor, then signals the condition, then waits for them to shut down. The race happens when a TaskExecutor thread checks `isShuttingDown` and finds it false, but before it can enter `await()`, the shutdown thread sets the flag and sends the signal—so the waiting thread misses it. So the lock coordination actually works correctly—the shutdown flag gets set before signaling, and the await releases the lock so the signal can proceed. But there's another potential issue: if the TaskExecutor catches an InterruptedException while waiting, it returns with the interrupted flag set, then the run loop catches that exception and calls awaitProcessableTasks again, which could create a loop.\n\nHowever, the isShuttingDown check prevents this—once shutdown is flagged, the method returns immediately without waiting. But for pause scenarios where interruption happens without a shutdown flag, if a signal was sent during the interruption window, it might get lost when the executor re-enters the wait. could be missed.\n\nActually, let me look at the actual race more carefully for the pause case:\n1. Some thread signals tasksCondition (for pause or task assignment)\n2. The TaskExecutor is NOT in await (it's processing the interruption)\n3. The signal is lost\n4. The TaskExecutor re-enters awaitProcessableTasks\n5. It checks for processable tasks - if there are none, it enters await\n6. But the signal from step 1 was already sent and lost\n7. The TaskExecutor waits forever\n\nIs this a real scenario? If the signal was for a task becoming processable, and the task is still processable when the executor re-enters the method, the executor would find the processable task in the for loop and not enter await. So the signal loss wouldn't cause a hang.\n\nBut what if the task became processable momentarily and then became unprocessable again? I can construct a specific interleaving where one executor picks up a task that was signaled as processable, and by the time another executor re-enters the wait loop, that task is already assigned and no new signals arrive—creating a genuine deadlock scenario. The mechanism that should prevent this is when an executor finishes with a task, it calls unassignTask which signals the condition again, but there's a window where this protection could fail. So the signal loss doesn't actually cause a hang because the condition gets rechecked before waiting—that's the standard safe pattern. But there's a real deadlock issue here: `assignNextTask` grabs `tasksLock` then tries to acquire `topologyLock` through `isTaskTopologyStale`, while `registerAndBuildNewTopology` does the opposite, acquiring `topologyLock` first and then needing `tasksLock`.\n\nNow I need to look at the `DefaultTaskExecutor` to find where the hang might be occurring.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":6287,"cache_read_input_tokens":118345,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":6287,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"385b3ce2-4bf0-4c25-8575-4b3d9846043b","uuid":"105a749b-901d-4efe-93f1-9d661deddc9c"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_01SmGduWvTuJUkwLj8Jxu9f7","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"OK, now I've confirmed the deadlock:\n\n**DEADLOCK in DefaultTaskManager:**\n\n- Path 1: `assignNextTask()` holds `tasksLock`, then calls `topologyMetadata.isTaskTopologyStale()` which acquires `topologyLock`.\n  - Lock order: `tasksLock` → `topologyLock`\n\n- Path 2: `TopologyMetadata.registerAndBuildNewTopology()` holds `topologyLock`, calls `notifyLifecycleObservers()` → `DefaultTaskManager.validateTopologyConsistency()` → `executeWithTasksLocked()` which acquires `tasksLock`.\n  - Lock order: `topologyLock` → `tasksLock`\n\nThis is a classic lock ordering inversion deadlock. Now I need to find the HANG bug. The description mentions potential missed condition signals or lost wakeups, so I'm examining the `awaitProcessableTasks` method to see how it's checking for available tasks and handling the wait/notify pattern.\n\nExamining the task executor's run loop...\n\nThe executor repeatedly calls `runOnce()`, which first tries to assign the next available task from the task manager. If no task is available, it blocks by calling `awaitProcessableTasks()` until either a task becomes available or the shutdown signal is received. Once a task is assigned, it processes it, and if the task doesn't make progress, it gets unassigned so another executor can try it.\n\nNow I'm checking whether there's a gap where a task becomes processable but no signal gets sent to wake up waiting threads. Looking at what makes a task processable—either it has records to process or it can punctuate—I need to trace through the record fetching flow to see if all paths properly signal when records arrive.\n\nActually, I think I found the issue. The `awaitProcessableTasks` method checks for processable tasks under the lock, and if none exist, it waits. But when it wakes up from the wait, it just returns immediately without rechecking whether any tasks actually became processable. The caller then tries to assign the next task, which could fail if the signal was spurious or if the condition changed.\n\nThe real problem is the lost wakeup scenario described in the code comment—if a thread gets interrupted while waiting on the condition, it might miss the shutdown signal that wakes all threads, causing it to block forever in the await call.\n\nBut I'm wondering if there's a similar race condition outside of shutdown. Let me trace through what happens when a task becomes processable while all executors are waiting: the signal gets sent, one executor wakes up and claims the task, processes it, then signals again when done. That cycle seems sound. But what if a task becomes processable right after an executor checks for processable tasks but before it calls await—could the signal be lost then?\n\nActually, there's another scenario I should consider: what if an external event makes a task processable without triggering `signalTaskExecutors()`? For instance, if the consumer pushes new records into the task's input buffer but the signal isn't called, all executors could hang indefinitely. This would depend on how the external code integrates with the stream thread, so I need to look more carefully at the unassign flow.\n\nExamining the unassign method...\n\nExamining the task release handling...\n\nThe flow is: Thread A calls `unassign()` on executor B, which sets `taskReleaseRequested` to a future and signals all executors. When executor B wakes from `await()`, it calls `handleTaskReleaseRequested()` at the start of `runOnce`, which retrieves and completes the future with the unassigned task. But I need to think through what happens if executor B is already running and not waiting.\n\nIf executor B is in the middle of processing a task when the signal fires, the signal is lost since B isn't in `await()`. However, this isn't actually a problem because B will check `taskReleaseRequested` in its next `runOnce` iteration and complete the future then. The only downside is latency if B's current task takes a long time.\n\nNow I'm reconsidering the hang scenario from scratch—the problem statement mentions operations hanging indefinitely, so there must be something deeper I'm missing about the synchronization logic.\n\nLooking at the `runOnce()` loop structure, I see it calls `handleTaskReleaseRequested()` first, then tries to assign a task, and if none is available it calls `awaitProcessableTasks()` to wait for new work. The issue is that `InterruptedException` from the await is silently caught and ignored, which could cause the executor to spin or miss important state changes.\n\nThe key insight is that the interrupt flag gets cleared when `InterruptedException` is caught, so the shutdown check relies entirely on the `shutdownRequested` flag rather than thread interruption status. The loop will continue spinning through `runOnce()` until `shutdownRequested` is actually checked in the outer while condition, but there's a window where the thread could be stuck or behaving unexpectedly if the shutdown signal and interrupt don't align properly. I'm seeing a potential race condition here—if a `signalAll()` is sent while the thread is handling the interrupt, that signal gets lost, and the thread ends up waiting in `await()` without ever receiving another notification to wake up.\n\nLet me trace through the `awaitProcessableTasks` method to understand the exact flow...\n\nThe method checks `isShuttingDown` before calling `await()`, so if shutdown is signaled, it won't enter the wait. But if a signal arrives while the thread is already in `await()`, it wakes up and returns `false` — meaning the caller proceeds to `runOnce` and `assignNextTask` without rechecking the shutdown condition. That could be the deadlock: the thread wakes from the signal but never verifies whether it should actually be shutting down before continuing. not after. So after waking up from `await()`, it returns `false`, and the caller re-enters the loop. The outer `while (!shutdownRequested.get())` check in the executor thread will catch the shutdown.\n\nHmm, actually I think the potential hang is in the following scenario:\n\n1. Thread is in `await()` \n2. Thread is interrupted → `InterruptedException`\n3. `awaitProcessableTasks` returns with `interrupted = true` → throws `InterruptedException`\n4. Caught in `runOnce` → ignored\n5. Thread re-enters `runOnce` → checks `handleTaskReleaseRequested` → nothing\n6. `assignNextTask` → acquires `tasksLock`, checks tasks → no processable task\n7. `awaitProcessableTasks` → acquires `tasksLock`, checks tasks → no processable task\n8. `isShuttingDown.get()` → false\n9. `tasksCondition.await()` → waiting\n\nThe issue is that if a signal was sent while the thread was interrupted between steps 2 and 9, that signal gets lost because the thread wasn't waiting yet. Once it enters `await()`, it's stuck waiting for a signal that already happened. In a typical Kafka Streams application this wouldn't be a problem since tasks are constantly being added or removed, generating new signals, but if the system reaches a quiescent state with no more activity, the thread could hang indefinitely.\n\nLet me trace through a concrete scenario with two executors and one task to see if this actually causes a hang.\n\nActually, I realize the condition check before `await()` catches this—when E2 re-enters `awaitProcessableTasks` after the interrupt, it checks if any tasks are processable, and if T1 was just unassigned, E2 will find it without needing to wait. The hang only happens if a task becomes processable and then becomes unprocessable again before the thread checks, which is a narrow edge case. For instance, if T1 becomes processable and triggers a signal, but E2 is still handling the interrupt, then another executor picks up T1 before E2 re-enters the check—now E2 finds no processable tasks and enters `await()` without anyone to signal it.\n\nBut even then, E1 will eventually finish processing T1 and signal again, so E2 wakes up and continues. The condition check before `await()` prevents permanent hangs in practice.\n\nNow I'm looking at another potential hang point in `TopologyMetadata.maybeWaitForNonEmptyTopology`, which has a lock-based wait mechanism that could deadlock if the topology never becomes non-empty while the thread is still alive. I need to check whether `wakeupThreads()` gets called during KafkaStreams shutdown, since that's the only way the waiting thread would be signaled if the state becomes non-alive without a new topology being registered.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":8419,"cache_read_input_tokens":124632,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":8419,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"385b3ce2-4bf0-4c25-8575-4b3d9846043b","uuid":"bf13e7c8-2a72-46ef-adb4-4e4afd710ba1"}

ProcessingMode processingMode;\n    private final TopologyVersion version;\n    private final TaskExecutionMetadata taskExecutionMetadata;\n    private final Set<String> pausedTopologies;\n\n    private final ConcurrentNavigableMap<String, InternalTopologyBuilder> builders; // Keep sorted by topology name for readability\n    private final List<TopologyLifecycleObserver> lifecycleObservers = HashSet<>();\n    private final Map<String, Long> threadVersions = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();\n\n    public static class TopologyVersion {\n        public AtomicLong topologyVersion = new AtomicLong(0L); // the local topology version\n        public ReentrantLock topologyLock = new ReentrantLock();\n        public Condition topologyCV = topologyLock.newCondition();\n        public List<TopologyVersionListener> activeTopologyUpdateListeners = new LinkedList<>();\n    }\n\n    public static class TopologyVersionListener {\n        final long topologyVersion; // the (minimum) version to wait for these threads to cross\n        final KafkaFutureImpl<Void> future; // the future waiting on all threads to be updated\n\n        public TopologyVersionListener(final long topologyVersion, final KafkaFutureImpl<Void> future) {\n            this.topologyVersion = topologyVersion;\n            this.future = future;\n        }\n    }\n\n    public interface TopologyLifecycleObserver {\n        void onTopologyChange(long newVersion, Set<String> activeTopologies);\n    }\n\n    public TopologyMetadata(final InternalTopologyBuilder builder,\n                            final StreamsConfig config) {\n        this.version = new TopologyVersion();\n        this.processingMode = StreamsConfigUtils.processingMode(config);\n        this.config = config;\n        this.log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(getClass());\n        this.pausedTopologies = ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet();\n\n        builders = new ConcurrentSkipListMap<>();\n        if (builder.hasNamedTopology()) {\n            builders.put(builder.topologyName(), builder);\n        } else {\n            builders.put(UNNAMED_TOPOLOGY, builder);\n        }\n        this.taskExecutionMetadata = new TaskExecutionMetadata(builders.keySet(), pausedTopologies, processingMode);\n    }\n\n    public TopologyMetadata(final ConcurrentNavigableMap<String, InternalTopologyBuilder> builders,\n                            final StreamsConfig config) {\n        this.version = new TopologyVersion();\n        this.processingMode = StreamsConfigUtils.processingMode(config);\n        this.config = config;\n        this.log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(getClass());\n        this.pausedTopologies = ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet();\n\n        this.builders = builders;\n        if (builders.isEmpty()) {\n            log.info(\"Created an empty KafkaStreams app with no topology\");\n        }\n        this.taskExecutionMetadata = new TaskExecutionMetadata(builders.keySet(), pausedTopologies, processingMode);\n    }\n\n    // Need to (re)set the log here to pick up the `processId` part of the clientId in the prefix\n    public void setLog(final LogContext logContext) {\n        log = logContext.logger(getClass());\n    }\n    \n    public ProcessingMode processingMode() {\n        return processingMode;\n    }\n\n    public long topologyVersion() {\n        return version.topologyVersion.get();\n    }\n\n    private void lock() {\n        version.topologyLock.lock();\n    }\n\n    private void unlock() {\n        version.topologyLock.unlock();\n    }\n\n    public Collection<String> sourceTopicsForTopology(final String name) {\n        return builders.get(name).fullSourceTopicNames();\n    }\n\n    public boolean needsUpdate(final String threadName) {\n        return threadVersions.get(threadName) < topologyVersion();\n    }\n\n    public void registerThread(final String threadName) {\n        threadVersions.put(threadName, 0L);\n    }\n\n    public void unregisterThread(final String threadName) {\n        threadVersions.remove(threadName);\n        maybeNotifyTopologyVersionListeners();\n    }\n\n    public TaskExecutionMetadata taskExecutionMetadata() {\n        return taskExecutionMetadata;\n    }\n\n    public void executeTopologyUpdatesAndBumpThreadVersion(final Consumer<Set<String>> handleTopologyAdditions,\n                                                           final Consumer<Set<String>> handleTopologyRemovals) {\n        try {\n            version.topologyLock.lock();\n            final long latestTopologyVersion = topologyVersion();\n            handleTopologyAdditions.accept(namedTopologiesView());\n            handleTopologyRemovals.accept(namedTopologiesView());\n            threadVersions.put(Thread.currentThread().getName(), latestTopologyVersion);\n        } finally {\n            version.topologyLock.unlock();\n        }\n    }\n\n    public void maybeNotifyTopologyVersionListeners() {\n        try {\n            lock();\n            final long minThreadVersion = minimumThreadVersion();\n            final Iterator<TopologyVersionListener> iterator = version.activeTopologyUpdateListeners.listIterator();\n            TopologyVersionListener topologyVersionListener;\n            while (iterator.hasNext()) {\n                topologyVersionListener = iterator.next();\n                final long topologyVersionWaitersVersion = topologyVersionListener.topologyVersion;\n                if (minThreadVersion >= topologyVersionWaitersVersion) {\n                    topologyVersionListener.future.complete(null);\n                    iterator.remove();\n                    log.info(\"All threads are now on topology version {}\", topologyVersionListener.topologyVersion);\n                }\n            }\n        } finally {\n            unlock();\n        }\n    }\n\n    // Return the minimum version across all live threads, or Long.MAX_VALUE if there are no threads running\n    private long minimumThreadVersion() {\n        final Optional<Long> minVersion = threadVersions.values().stream().min(Long::compare);\n        return minVersion.orElse(Long.MAX_VALUE);\n    }\n\n    public void wakeupThreads() {\n        try {\n            lock();\n            version.topologyCV.signalAll();\n        } finally {\n            unlock();\n        }\n    }\n\n    public void registerLifecycleObserver(final TopologyLifecycleObserver observer) {\n        lock();\n        try {\n            lifecycleObservers.add(observer);\n        } finally {\n            unlock();\n        }\n    }\n\n    private void notifyLifecycleObservers() {\n        final long currentVersion = version.topologyVersion.get();\n        final Set<String> activeTopologies = namedTopologiesView();\n        for (final TopologyLifecycleObserver observer : lifecycleObservers) {\n            try {\n                observer.onTopologyChange(currentVersion, activeTopologies);\n            } catch (final Exception e) {\n                log.warn(\"Topology lifecycle observer threw exception\", e);\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    public boolean isTaskTopologyStale(final TaskId taskId) {\n        lock();\n        try {\n            final String topologyName = taskId.topologyName();\n            if (topologyName != null) {\n                return !builders.containsKey(topologyName);\n            }\n            return false;\n        } finally {\n            unlock();\n        }\n    }\n\n    public void maybeWaitForNonEmptyTopology(final Supplier<StreamThread.State> threadState) {\n        if (isEmpty() && threadState.get().isAlive()) {\n            try {\n                lock();\n                while (isEmpty() && threadState.get().isAlive()) {\n                    try {\n                        log.debug(\"Detected that the topology is currently empty, waiting for something to process\");\n                        version.topologyCV.await();\n                    } catch (final InterruptedException e) {\n                        log.error(\"StreamThread was interrupted while waiting on empty topology\", e);\n                    }\n                }\n            } finally {\n                unlock();\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Adds the topology and registers a future that listens for all threads on the older version to see the update\n     */\n    public void registerAndBuildNewTopology(final KafkaFutureImpl<Void> future, final InternalTopologyBuilder newTopologyBuilder) {\n        try {\n            lock();\n            buildAndVerifyTopology(newTopologyBuilder);\n            log.info(\"New NamedTopology {} passed validation and will be added, old topology version is {}\", newTopologyBuilder.topologyName(), version.topologyVersion.get());\n            version.topologyVersion.incrementAndGet();\n            version.activeTopologyUpdateListeners.add(new TopologyVersionListener(topologyVersion(), future));\n            builders.put(newTopologyBuilder.topologyName(), newTopologyBuilder);\n            wakeupThreads();\n            notifyLifecycleObservers();\n            log.info(\"Added NamedTopology {} and updated topology version to {}\", newTopologyBuilder.topologyName(), version.topologyVersion.get());\n        } catch (final Throwable throwable) {\n            log.error(\"Failed to add NamedTopology {}, please retry the operation.\", newTopologyBuilder.topologyName());\n            future.completeExceptionally(throwable);\n        } finally {\n            unlock();\n        }\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Pauses a topology by name\n     * @param topologyName Name of the topology to pause\n     */\n    public void pauseTopology(final String topologyName) {\n        pausedTopologies.add(topologyName);\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Checks if a given topology is paused.\n     * @param topologyName If null, assume that we are checking the `UNNAMED_TOPOLOGY`.\n     * @return A boolean indicating if the topology is paused.\n     */\n    public boolean isPaused(final String topologyName) {\n        return pausedTopologies.contains(getTopologyNameOrElseUnnamed(topologyName));\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Resumes a topology by name\n     * @param topologyName Name of the topology to resume\n     */\n    public void resumeTopology(final String topologyName) {\n        pausedTopologies.remove(topologyName);\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Removes the topology and registers a future that listens for all threads on the older version to see the update\n     */\n    public KafkaFuture<Void> unregisterTopology(final KafkaFutureImpl<Void> removeTopologyFuture,\n                                                final String topologyName) {\n        try {\n            lock();\n            log.info(\"Beginning removal of NamedTopology {}, old topology version is {}\", topologyName, version.topologyVersion.get());\n            version.topologyVersion.incrementAndGet();\n            version.activeTopologyUpdateListeners.add(new TopologyVersionListener(topologyVersion(), removeTopologyFuture));\n            final InternalTopologyBuilder removedBuilder = builders.remove(topologyName);\n            removedBuilder.fullSourceTopicNames().forEach(allInputTopics::remove);\n            removedBuilder.allSourcePatternStrings().forEach(allInputTopics::remove);\n            notifyLifecycleObservers();\n            log.info(\"Finished removing NamedTopology {}, topology version was updated to {}\", topologyName, version.topologyVersion.get());\n        } catch (final Throwable throwable) {\n            log.error(\"Failed to remove NamedTopology {}, please retry.\", topologyName);\n            removeTopologyFuture.completeExceptionally(throwable);\n        } finally {\n            unlock();\n        }\n        return removeTopologyFuture;\n    }\n\n    public TaskConfig taskConfig(final TaskId taskId) {\n        final InternalTopologyBuilder builder = lookupBuilderForTask(taskId);\n        return builder.topologyConfigs().getTaskConfig();\n    }\n\n    public void buildAndRewriteTopology() {\n        applyToEachBuilder(this::buildAndVerifyTopology);\n    }\n\n    private void buildAndVerifyTopology(final InternalTopologyBuilder builder) {\n        builder.rewriteTopology(config);\n        builder.buildTopology();\n\n        final Set<String> allInputTopicsCopy = new HashSet<>(allInputTopics);\n\n        // As we go, check each topology for overlap in the set of input topics/patterns\n        final int numInputTopics = allInputTopicsCopy.size();\n        final List<String> inputTopics = builder.fullSourceTopicNames();\n        final Collection<String> inputPatterns = builder.allSourcePatternStrings();\n\n        final Set<String> newInputTopics = new HashSet<>(inputTopics);\n        newInputTopics.addAll(inputPatterns);\n\n        final int numNewInputTopics = newInputTopics.size();\n        allInputTopicsCopy.addAll(newInputTopics);\n\n        if (allInputTopicsCopy.size() != numInputTopics + numNewInputTopics) {\n            inputTopics.retainAll(allInputTopicsCopy);\n            inputPatterns.retainAll(allInputTopicsCopy);\n            log.error(\"Tried to add the NamedTopology {} but it had overlap with other input topics {} or patterns {}\",\n                      builder.topologyName(), inputTopics, inputPatterns);\n            throw new TopologyException(\"Named Topologies may not subscribe to the same input topics or patterns\");\n        }\n\n        final ProcessorTopology globalTopology = builder.buildGlobalStateTopology();\n        if (globalTopology != null) {\n            if (builder.topologyName() != null) {\n                throw new IllegalStateException(\"Global state stores are not supported with Named Topologies\");\n            } else if (this.globalTopology != null) {\n                throw new TopologyException(\"Topology builder had global state, but global topology has already been set\");\n            } else {\n                this.globalTopology = globalTopology;\n                globalStateStores.putAll(builder.globalStateStores());\n            }\n        }\n        allInputTopics.addAll(newInputTopics);\n    }\n\n    public int numStreamThreads(final StreamsConfig config) {\n        final int configuredNumStreamThreads = config.getInt(StreamsConfig.NUM_STREAM_THREADS_CONFIG);\n\n        // If there are named topologies but some are empty, this indicates a bug in user code\n        if (hasNamedTopologies()) {\n            if (hasNoLocalTopology()) {\n                log.error(\"Detected a named topology with no input topics, a named topology may not be empty.\");\n                throw new TopologyException(\"Topology has no stream threads and no global threads, \" +\n                                                \"must subscribe to at least one source topic or pattern.\");\n            }\n        } else {\n            // If both the global and non-global topologies are empty, this indicates a bug in user code\n            if (hasNoLocalTopology() && !hasGlobalTopology()) {\n                log.error(\"Topology with no input topics will create no stream threads and no global thread.\");\n                throw new TopologyException(\"Topology has no stream threads and no global threads, \" +\n                                                \"must subscribe to at least one source topic or global table.\");\n            }\n        }\n\n        // Lastly we check for an empty non-global topology and override the threads to zero if set otherwise\n        if (configuredNumStreamThreads != 0 && hasNoLocalTopology()) {\n            log.info(\"Overriding number of StreamThreads to zero for global-only topology\");\n            return 0;\n        }\n\n        return configuredNumStreamThreads;\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * @return true iff the app is using named topologies, or was started up with no topology at all\n     */\n    public boolean hasNamedTopologies() {\n        return !builders.containsKey(UNNAMED_TOPOLOGY);\n    }\n\n    public Set<String> namedTopologiesView() {\n        return hasNamedTopologies() ? Collections.unmodifiableSet(builders.keySet()) : emptySet();\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * @return true iff any of the topologies have a global topology\n     */\n    public boolean hasGlobalTopology() hasPersistentStores() {\n        // If the app is using named topologies, there may not be any persistent state when it first starts up\n        // but a new NamedTopology may introduce it later, so we must return true\n        if (hasNamedTopologies()) {\n            return true;\n        }\n        return evaluateConditionIsTrueForAnyBuilders(InternalTopologyBuilder::hasPersistentStores);\n    }\n\n    public boolean hasStore(final String name) {\n        return evaluateConditionIsTrueForAnyBuilders(b -> b.hasStore(name));\n    }\n\n    public boolean hasOffsetResetOverrides() {\n        // evaluateConditionIsTrueForAnyBuilders(InternalTopologyBuilder::hasOffsetResetOverrides);\n    }\n\n    public Optional<AutoOffsetResetStrategy> offsetResetStrategy(final String topic) {\n        for (final InternalTopologyBuilder builder : builders.values()) {\n            if (builder.containsTopic(topic)) {\n                return Optional.ofNullable(builder.offsetResetStrategy(topic));\n            }\n        }\n        log.warn(\"Unable to look up offset reset strategy for topic {} \" +\n            \"as this topic does not appear in the sources of any of the current topologies: {}\\n \" +\n                \"This may be due to natural race condition when removing a topology but it should not \" +\n                \"persist or appear frequently.\",\n            topic, when we remove NamedTopologies, we can remove this exception\n        return null;\n    }\n\n\n    public Collection<String> fullSourceTopicNamesForTopology(final String topologyName) {\n sourceTopics;\n    }\n\n    Pattern sourceTopicPattern() {\n        final StringBuilder patternBuilder = new StringBuilder();\n\n        applyToEachBuilder(b -> {\n            final String if (patternBuilder.length() > 0) {\n            patternBuilder.setLength(patternBuilder.length() - 1);\n            return Pattern.compile(patternBuilder.toString());\n        } else {\n            return EMPTY_ZERO_LENGTH_PATTERN;\n        }\n    }\n\n    public boolean usesPatternSubscription() {\n        return   */\n    public ProcessorTopology buildSubtopology(final TaskId task) {\n        final InternalTopologyBuilder builder = lookupBuilderForTask(task);\n        return final String topologyName) {\n        return lookupBuilderForNamedTopology(topologyName).sourceTopicsForStore(storeName);\n    }\n\n    public static String getTopologyNameOrElseUnnamed(final String topologyName) {\n        return topologyName == null ? UNNAMED_TOPOLOGY : topologyName;\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * @param topologiesToExclude the names of any topologies to exclude from the returned topic groups,\n     *                            eg public Map<Subtopology, TopicsInfo> subtopologyTopicsInfoMapExcluding(final Set<String> topologiesToExclude) {\n        final Map<Subtopology, TopicsInfo> subtopologyTopicsInfo = new HashMap<>();\n      for (final InternalTopologyBuilder builder : builders.values()) {\n            if (condition.apply(builder)) {\n                return true;\n            }\n        }\n        return false;\n test to detect deadlock between DefaultTaskManager.tasksLock and TopologyMetadata.topologyLock.\n *\n * Deadlock scenario:\n *   Thread 1: assignNextTask() -> tasksLock -> isTaskTopologyStale() -> topologyLock\n *   Thread 2: registerAndBuildNewTopology() -> topologyLock -> notifyLifecycleObservers() -> tasksLock\n */\npublic class FrayDeadlockTest {\n\n    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {\n        final Time time = new MockTime();\n        final Properties props = new Properties();\n        props.put(StreamsConfig.APPLICATION_ID_CONFIG, \"test-app\");\n        props.put(StreamsConfig.BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_CONFIG, \"localhost:9092\");\n        final StreamsConfig config = new StreamsConfig(props);\n\n        final ConcurrentNavigableMap<String, InternalTopologyBuilder> builders = new ConcurrentSkipListMap<>();\n        final TopologyMetadata topologyMetadata = new TopologyMetadata(builders, config);\n\n        // Create a minimal TasksRegistry that returns empty sets\n        final TasksRegistry tasksRegistry = new EmptyTasksRegistry();\n\n        // Create a minimal TaskExecutionMetadata\n        final TaskExecutionMetadata taskExecutionMetadata = new TaskExecutionMetadata(\n            Collections.emptySet(), Collections.emptySet(),\n            org.apache.kafka.streams.internals.StreamsConfigUtils.ProcessingMode.AT_LEAST_ONCE\n        );\n\n        // Create a no-op executor creator\n        final TaskExecutorCreator executorCreator = (tm, name, t, tem) ->\n            new NoOpTaskExecutor(name);\n\n        final DefaultTaskManager taskManager = new DefaultTaskManager(\n            time, \"test-client\", tasksRegistry, executorCreator, taskExecutionMetadata, 1, topologyMetadata\n        );\n\n        // Thread 1: simulates assignNextTask path (tasksLock -> topologyLock)\n        final Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> {\n            for org.apache.kafka.streams.processor.internals.namedtopology.NamedTopology(topoName)\n                );\n                try {\n                    topologyMetadata.registerAndBuildNewTopology(new KafkaFutureImpl<>(), builder);\n                } catch (final Exception e) {\n                    // ignore build failures; the lock ordering is what we're testing\n                }\n            }\n activeInitializedTasks() { return Collections.emptySet(); }\n        @Override public boolean containsInitialized(final TaskId taskId) { return false; }\n        @Override public Task initializedTask(final TaskId taskId) { return null; }\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Minimal TaskExecutor that does nothing.\n     */\n    static class NoOpTaskExecutor implements TaskExecutor {\n @Override public boolean isRunning() { return false; }\n        @Override public void requestShutdown() {}\n        @Override public void awaitShutdown(Duration timeout) {}\n        @Override public }\n}\n"}}
